title: What Has Arisen in Tamil Nadu Is Not Blossoming, but a People's Uprising!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. This letter answers statements made during Jayalalithaa's Virudhachalam campaign. Allegations concerning UDAY, the Western Ghats and LED procurement remain explicitly attributed to the Union ministers Kalaignar quotes; they are not converted into unqualified narrative claims. The Tamil reading copy below repairs only unambiguous OCR defects. The canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Continuing the lies she told at her Chennai election campaign meeting, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa did not fail to tell as many lies as she could, in her customary manner, at her Virudhachalam campaign meeting too. She said that certain Union Ministers and DMK leader Karunanidhi had been criticising the Tamil Nadu Government for not joining the Union Government's UDAY scheme.

I merely cited the criticism made by a Union Minister about Tamil Nadu's failure to join UDAY and asked what answer the Chief Minister had. I expressed no view either supporting or opposing the scheme. When I said no such thing, why did Jayalalithaa wage a shadow war?
Union Minister Javadekar said in an interview:

> “The Tamil Nadu Electricity Board suffered a loss of about Rs 12,000 crore last year alone. Electricity theft is increasing. Even so, the State Government has not come forward to implement the Union Government's UDAY scheme, which would help rescue Tamil Nadu's electricity sector from losses and increase revenue. Other States implementing the scheme have obtained good results. Its implementation in other States has yielded benefits worth Rs 1.80 lakh crore. But the Tamil Nadu Government has no concern for public welfare. It does not want honest consumers to benefit.”

I cited what the Union Minister had said and wrote only that the Chief Minister must offer an explanation. In my statement, I did not say that the Tamil Nadu Government should accept UDAY. I asked only for its explanation of the allegations Union Ministers had made against it concerning the scheme.

Jayalalithaa has now offered an explanation. Why did she not answer immediately after the Union Minister's interview? Had she done so, the issue would never have arisen. Why did she keep her mouth shut then and go to Virudhachalam now to pounce upon me? Because she had no other subject to discuss, Jayalalithaa imagined that I had said something I had not and replied to that invention.
Yesterday, 13 April 2016, Union Minister Piyush Goyal was in Tiruchi. When asked about Jayalalithaa's position, he said:

> “Because of the State Government's obstinacy, I could not meet Chief Minister Jayalalithaa. I sent a committee headed by a Joint Secretary to resolve the Tamil Nadu Government's doubts about this scheme. They clearly answered the questions raised by the State Government. Jayalalithaa's explanation concerning UDAY is unsupported and incorrect.”

The Union Minister thus made his position clear. I said only that, when the Union Minister and Tamil Nadu Chief Minister were raising questions back and forth about the scheme, should its true position not be explained to the people?
Speaking to journalists in Tiruchi on 13 April 2016, BJP National President Amit Shah said:

> “The Narendra Modi Government at the Centre brought many achievement-oriented schemes to Tamil Nadu. But the State Government did not cooperate. Schemes such as the Prime Minister's crop-insurance programme and insurance for the poor did not reach working people because of the State Government's lack of cooperation. Tamil Nadu is the most corrupt State in India. The most corrupt Government operates in Tamil Nadu.”
When Amit Shah has levelled these allegations against the Tamil Nadu Government, should Chief Minister Jayalalithaa not give an appropriate answer at least to him?

Not one but two or three Union Ministers have repeatedly said that they could not meet Chief Minister Jayalalithaa. Jayalalithaa glossed over this by saying Venkaiah Naidu had met her. At Virudhachalam, she also said that O. Panneerselvam had already issued a statement listing when Union Ministers met her, and claimed that this was the answer. But O. Panneerselvam's statement merely listed occasions when Chief Minister Jayalalithaa met Union Ministers at ceremonies and other events. It did not say when she met Union Ministers to discuss Tamil Nadu's needs. Jayalalithaa conveniently concealed that fact.

Jayalalithaa also said that Karunanidhi's claim that Tamil Nadu had still not submitted a report on the protection of the Western Ghats was a false allegation. I never made that allegation against her either. What I said was that the Union Minister had further stated:

> “Reports concerning protection of the Western Ghats were sought from the seven surrounding States, including Tamil Nadu. Six States submitted reports to the Union Government. The Tamil Nadu Government alone has so far provided neither a report nor a reply. I tried more than a hundred times to meet the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister, but could not.”

I said only that this was no ordinary allegation but an extremely serious charge made by a Union Minister against the Tamil Nadu Government and Chief Minister Jayalalithaa. If there are old newspapers in her office, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa would do well to examine them and at least now understand who made the allegation.

Set aside Jayalalithaa's refusal to meet Union Ministers. Jayalalithaa flies out to campaign only at election time. During these five years, how many times did she personally visit the districts and meet and speak with the people?
At Virudhachalam, Jayalalithaa said that I had become confused and asked questions about LED bulbs. I was not confused about them. A Union Minister said:

> “The Union Government supplies LED bulbs to State Governments at a price of one hundred rupees. Even so, the Tamil Nadu Government did not cooperate with this scheme. Instead, it purchases LED bulbs on the open market. This proves that the Tamil Nadu Government is acting in favour of those who sell them at higher prices.”

The Union Minister thus made a documented allegation of corruption against the Tamil Nadu Government. In my statement, I asked only what explanation the Government had. Without learning what English newspapers and *Junior Vikatan* had written about LED bulbs, and without even properly reading the material I quoted from them, Jayalalithaa arrogantly declared that I did not understand the scheme and that she knew everything. It would be easy to reply in the same manner. But that would not befit my age or experience.

Until the election ends, Jayalalithaa will melt before the people to obtain their votes, saying, “I am because of you; I am for you.” But the people of Tamil Nadu have not forgotten that, after the election ended and she had won and seated herself on the throne, she climbed to the very tip of arrogance and danced in authoritarian fashion, saying, “My Government; I ordered it,” and “From now on, everything is I, I, I alone.”
At the Virudhachalam public meeting, she played the role of a mother, saying: “A mother knows what her child needs. Therefore, this mother knows what must be done for you.”
When the mothers of Tamil Nadu spontaneously protested demanding the closure of TASMAC shops, the police unleashed violence upon them and arrested them. Is that how a mother treats her children?

Because of power cuts and electricity shortages, girls preparing for examinations could not study properly and their education was ruined. Is that the help a mother provides after understanding the needs of her daughters?

Despite court orders, she dismissed twenty thousand public-welfare workers without the slightest concern, causing thousands of mothers and girls to cry inconsolably. Was that the gift a mother knowingly gave her children?

Under the rule of this “mother,” 2,335 women suffered sexual violence in the past three years alone, and more than twenty thousand crimes were committed against women. Are these proofs of her maternal quality? Why did Dalit woman police officer Vishnupriya, who served under this “mother,” die by suicide?

In a man-made disaster across Chennai and neighbouring districts, several lakh mothers lost their homes and possessions, became refugees, and were driven into unbearable suffering. Did that too happen with this mother's knowledge? Will maternal qualities come running and attach themselves to someone merely because she calls herself a “mother” in empty words?

Jayalalithaa also asks, “What family do I have of my own?” There is nothing wrong with a person having a family. But does Jayalalithaa live alone? Do not her “sister not born with her” and that sister's numerous relatives all live in the Poes Garden mansion? Do they not exercise influence in many directions from there? When someone keeps not one but several families with her and asks, “What family do I have?”, do the people not regard it as sophistry?

I explained prohibition in detail, citing its historical evidence and the sequence of events. Yet Jayalalithaa either did not read it or concealed what she read. Like a trained parrot repeating the same thing at every meeting, she insists that the DMK Government repealed prohibition. Considering Tamil Nadu's financial crisis at the time, the Kazhagam Government only postponed prohibition, and that too for only a few years. In 1974, under the DMK Government itself, I reintroduced prohibition. The liquor shops operating now were first established under AIADMK rule.

But the person holding the office of Chief Minister sets aside this truth, travels from town to town, repeats the same lies, and seeks to deceive the people. Is that right? No one can surpass Jayalalithaa's qualification for telling lies.

In her election campaign, Jayalalithaa says that Tamil Nadu has experienced growth and blossoming. During these five years of Jayalalithaa's rule, what Tamil Nadu has experienced is not growth but a decline never seen before; not blossoming but a people's uprising on many fronts!
With affection,
M.K.
